title.en: Compute Engine & GKE: VMs to Kubernetes

description.en: Understand GCP's compute spectrum — machine families, Spot VMs, MIGs — and when to step up to GKE Standard or Autopilot for containerized workloads.

story_variants.en:
## The Compute Spectrum

Google Cloud gives you a ladder of compute abstractions. At the bottom is **Compute Engine**: raw virtual machines where you own the OS, patching, and scaling story. At the top are fully managed services like Cloud Run (next module). In the middle sits **Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE)** — managed Kubernetes for teams that need container orchestration with real control.

The enterprise question is never 'which is best' but 'how much operational responsibility do we want to keep?'

> **Callout:** Choose the highest abstraction that still satisfies your constraints. Every rung down the ladder you buy flexibility with your on-call rotation's sleep.

## Compute Engine: Machine Families Decoded

Machine type names encode family, series, and shape. `e2-standard-4` means: E2 family (cost-optimized), standard shape (4 GiB memory per vCPU), 4 vCPUs — so 4 vCPU / 16 GiB.

| Family | Optimized for | Typical use |
|---|---|---|
| E2 | Cost | Dev/test, small services, batch |
| N2 / N2D / N4 | Balanced general purpose | Most production services |
| C3 / C4 | Compute performance | High-QPS APIs, game servers |
| M-series | Memory (up to multi-TiB) | SAP HANA, in-memory DBs |
| A2 / A3 / G2 | Accelerators (NVIDIA GPUs) | ML training and inference |

Shapes within a family: `standard` (4 GiB/vCPU), `highmem` (8 GiB/vCPU), `highcpu` (1-2 GiB/vCPU). Memorize that ratio trick — it lets you size clusters in your head, which you will do in this lesson's challenge.

Other load-bearing Compute Engine concepts:

- **Spot VMs** — deeply discounted capacity that Google can preempt with a 30-second warning. Perfect for fault-tolerant batch and CI, catastrophic for stateful singletons.
- **Managed Instance Groups (MIGs)** — templates + autoscaling + autohealing for fleets of identical VMs. The pre-Kubernetes way to scale, still excellent for simple stateless tiers.
- **Custom machine types** — dial vCPU and memory independently when standard shapes waste money.
- **Sole-tenant nodes** — physical hosts dedicated to you, for licensing (BYOL) or compliance isolation.

## GKE: Kubernetes Without Running the Control Plane

GKE hosts the Kubernetes **control plane** for you (API server, etcd, scheduler) and manages **node pools** — groups of identical Compute Engine VMs that run your pods. Two operating modes matter:

- **GKE Standard**: you define node pools, machine types, and autoscaling ranges. You pay for the nodes whether pods fill them or not. Maximum control — GPUs, DaemonSets, custom kernels, local SSD.
- **GKE Autopilot**: Google manages nodes entirely; you pay per pod resource request. No node SSH, fewer footguns, security hardening by default. Since its promotion to the recommended mode, Autopilot is where new clusters should start unless you have a concrete blocker.

> **Pitfall:** In Standard mode, teams routinely run clusters at 20-30% utilization because node pools were sized for peak. Autopilot converts that waste into a per-pod bill; Standard requires you to actively manage bin-packing.

## The Sizing Math Every Platform Engineer Does

A node's CPU is not fully yours. GKE reserves resources for the kubelet, the OS, and eviction thresholds. The **allocatable** capacity is what the scheduler can actually place pods on. A workable mental model (and the one our challenge uses): reserve roughly one vCPU per node for the system, then divide the remainder by your pod's CPU request.

For an `e2-standard-4` node (4 vCPU) and pods requesting 1 vCPU each: about 3 pods per node fit. Ten nodes, thirty pods. This arithmetic — capacity, requests, bin-packing — is the daily bread of cluster operators, and it is exactly why **resource requests** on every pod are non-negotiable in production: the scheduler can only pack what it can measure.

GKE layers autoscaling on this at three levels:

- **Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA)** — more pod replicas when CPU/memory/custom metrics rise.
- **Cluster Autoscaler** — more nodes when pods are unschedulable (Standard mode).
- **Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA)** — right-sizes the requests themselves over time.

## Decision Guide: VM, MIG, or GKE?

- Single lift-and-shift Windows server with a licensed app? **Compute Engine**, maybe sole-tenant.
- Stateless fleet behind a load balancer, no containers, small team? **MIG** with an instance template.
- Dozens of containerized microservices, multiple teams, need service mesh, GPUs for ML sidecars? **GKE** — Autopilot first, Standard if you need node-level control.
- One containerized HTTP service and you'd rather not think about clusters at all? Hold that thought for the **Cloud Run** lesson.

Regional decisions matter too: a **regional** GKE cluster replicates the control plane across three zones — production default. **Zonal** clusters are cheaper for dev but a zone outage takes the API server with it.

> **Callout:** GKE also matters to this track's destination: Vertex AI training jobs and Ray-on-Vertex run on GPU machine families you now know how to read (a2-highgpu, a3-megagpu, g2-standard). The naming literacy transfers directly.

## What You'll Build

The challenge is a node-pool capacity calculator: parse a spec table, compute total vCPU/memory per pool, and estimate schedulable pods after system reserve. It is the back-of-the-envelope every GKE migration starts with — get it wrong and either pods sit Pending or finance asks why the cluster is idle.

description.en: Each step right trades control for lower operational burden.
GKE separates the managed control plane from node pools you scale.

left.label: GKE Standard: pay per node, manage pools, 25% avg utilization

right.label: GKE Autopilot: pay per pod request, Google runs the nodes

description.en: Autopilot is the recommended default for new clusters; Standard when you need node-level control.

animation_sequence.code:
name = "e2-standard-4"
family, shape, vcpu = name.split("-")
mem_per_vcpu = {"standard": 4, "highmem": 8, "highcpu": 1}
mem = int(vcpu) * mem_per_vcpu[shape]

explanation.en: Machine type names are structured: family-shape-vcpucount.
Splitting on '-' gives you the three components programmatically.
Each shape implies a GiB-per-vCPU ratio: standard=4, highmem=8, highcpu=1-2.
e2-standard-4 therefore has 4 vCPU and 16 GiB memory.

description.en: Pending pods with 'Insufficient cpu' means your capacity math was wrong — the challenge teaches you to do it right.

instruction.en: Build a GKE node-pool capacity calculator. Given MACHINE_SPECS mapping machine type to (vcpu, memory_gib) and POOLS as (machine_type, node_count, pod_cpu_request) tuples, implement pool_capacity(machine_type, node_count) returning (total_vcpu, total_mem_gib), and max_pods(machine_type, node_count, pod_cpu) returning schedulable pods assuming 1 vCPU per node is reserved for the system (allocatable = vcpu - 1, pods per node = allocatable // pod_cpu). For each pool, in order, print exactly: '<machine_type> x<count> -> <vcpu> vCPU, <mem> GiB, max <pods> pods'. Print nothing else.

practice_challenges.expected_output:
e2-standard-4 x3 -> 12 vCPU, 48 GiB, max 9 pods
e2-standard-8 x2 -> 16 vCPU, 64 GiB, max 6 pods
n2-highmem-4 x4 -> 16 vCPU, 128 GiB, max 12 pods

en: Look up (vcpu, mem) with MACHINE_SPECS[machine_type], then multiply each by node_count.
For max_pods: (vcpu - 1) // pod_cpu gives pods per node; use integer division //.
Use an f-string: f"{mt} x{count} -> {v} vCPU, {m} GiB, max {p} pods".

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert pool_capacity("e2-standard-4", 3) == (12, 48)
assert pool_capacity("n2-highmem-4", 4) == (16, 128)
assert max_pods("e2-standard-4", 3, 1) == 9
assert max_pods("e2-standard-8", 2, 2) == 6
assert max_pods("n2-highmem-4", 4, 1) == 12

q.en: What does the machine type name n2-highmem-8 tell you?

en: N2 family, high-memory shape (8 GiB per vCPU), 8 vCPUs — so 64 GiB

q.en: Which workload is a good fit for Spot VMs?

en: A stateless CI build fleet that tolerates 30-second preemption
A licensing server that must never restart

q.en: Key difference between GKE Autopilot and GKE Standard?

en: Autopilot does not run real Kubernetes
In Autopilot you pay for pod resource requests and Google manages nodes; in Standard you manage and pay for node pools

q.en: Pods are stuck Pending with 'Insufficient cpu' in a Standard cluster. Which mechanism adds nodes to fix this automatically?
